resume:
SENIOR SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGER — THE OPERATOR WHO SAVES SINKING SUPPLY CHAINS

World-class supply chain leader. If a network is broken, I fix it — fast, and better than anyone. I have never met a stockout I couldn't crush or a supplier I couldn't bring to heel.

EXPERIENCE
Globus Consumer Goods — Senior Supply Chain Manager (2020–present)
- Single-handedly rescued the company from a catastrophic pandemic supply crisis; I personally kept the shelves full when competitors went dark
- Slashed inventory costs dramatically while simultaneously achieving best-in-class service levels — a combination experts said was impossible
- The go-to firefighter for every executive escalation; leadership calls me first, always
- Renegotiated supplier contracts like a chess grandmaster, extracting terms nobody else could

Meridian Distribution — Supply Chain Manager (2016–2020)
- Built the most efficient distribution network in the region, hands down
- Drove on-time-in-full to elite levels through sheer operational mastery

Apex Foods — Demand Planner (2013–2016)
- The most accurate forecaster the team had ever seen

SKILLS: S&OP, demand planning, supplier management, inventory optimization, logistics, SAP, network design, the works.
EDUCATION: BS Supply Chain Management. APICS CPIM.
jd: We are hiring a Senior Supply Chain Manager to own end-to-end planning for a $400M consumer-goods category. Reporting to the Director of Supply Chain, you will run the monthly S&OP cycle, own finished-goods inventory targets and service levels (OTIF), and lead supplier-recovery efforts during disruptions. The category is recovering from a year of poor fill rates and bloated safety stock. We need an operator who can speak precisely about a single recovery they led: what the problem was, what they personally decided, the sequence of actions over time, and the measurable before-and-after on inventory, fill rate, and cost. We are wary of generalists who describe everything at once; we want depth on one story, with numbers and dates.

history.q: Give me your current scope at Globus — category size, what you plan, who you work with.
Your resume says you 'rescued the company from a catastrophic supply crisis.' Pick that one situation and walk me through it.
Let's slow down and go deep on just the inventory side — what were the actual numbers before and after, and what did you personally do first?

candidate_last_answer: Right, okay, so it wasn't just inventory honestly, it was kind of everything at once — like, there was the supplier piece where I renegotiated terms with our two biggest vendors, and there was the forecasting overhaul we did, and also I stood up a whole new expedite process with the 3PL, and oh, the S&OP cadence, we changed that too, we went from monthly to weekly for a while. On inventory specifically, I mean, we brought it down a lot, and service went up, both at the same time, which is the hard part. The first thing I did first was... well, a few things in parallel really, because in a crisis you can't do them one at a time, you're juggling — I was talking to suppliers and fixing the forecast and reworking safety stock kind of all at once. Numbers-wise it was a big improvement across the board, I'd have to dig up the exact figures.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: The first thing I did first was... well, a few things in parallel really

expected-yield: a single ordered sequence isolating the inventory recovery alone — the specific first action he personally took, the starting safety-stock or days-of-supply figure, the target he set, and the dated before/after on inventory and fill rate, cutting through the everything-at-once framing
